sections.header: Verses 1–2 — “I Have Calmed and Quieted Myself Like a Weaned Child”

heb.paragraph: "I have calmed and quieted myself, like a weaned child (gāmal) with its mother; like a weaned child I am content" (v.2). Three verses of humility. David’s heart is not proud, his eyes not haughty (v.1). He does not concern himself with great matters beyond him (v.1). The weaned child image: not nursing (demanding), but resting (content). The child no longer needs the breast; it rests simply because the mother is near. Presence, not provision, is enough.

notes.note: "My heart is not proud, LORD; my eyes are not haughty. I do not concern myself with great matters." Three negatives: not proud, not haughty, not overreaching. The psalm of chosen smallness.
"Like a weaned child with its mother." The weaned child has stopped demanding and started resting. Contentment is not getting what you want but wanting what you have: the mother’s presence.

sections.header: Verse 3 — “Israel, Put Your Hope in the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Israel, put your hope (yaḥēl) in the LORD both now and forevermore" (v.3). One verse of national application. David’s personal humility becomes the nation’s counsel: hope in the LORD. The smallest psalm of the Ascents (3 verses) carries one of the deepest theologies: contentment through chosen smallness.

notes.note: "Israel, put your hope in the LORD both now and forevermore." The personal (vv.1–2) becomes communal (v.3). David’s quieted soul models what Israel should be.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David’s quieted soul models what Israel should be…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMy heart is not proud, Lord, my eyes are not haughty; I do not concern myself with great matters or things too wonderful for me.ESVO LORD, my heart is not lifted up; my eyes are not raised too high; I do not occupy myself with things too great and too marvelous for me.NIVIsrael, put your hope in the Lord both now and forevermore.ESVO Israel, hope in the LORD from this time forth and forevermore.

rec.text: ""My heart is not proud, LORD; my eyes are not haughty. I do not concern myself with great matters." Three negatives: not proud, not haughty, not overreaching. The psalm of chosen smallness."

rows.label: Hope — 131:3
rows.text: "Israel, put your HOPE in the LORD." 3 verses. The psalm of chosen smallness and contentment.

hebtext.note: "I have calmed and quieted myself, like a weaned child (gāmal) with its mother; like a weaned child I am content" (v.2). Three verses of humility. David’s heart is not proud, his eyes not haughty (v.1). He does not concern himself with great matters beyond him (v.1). The weaned child image: not nursing (demanding), but resting (content). The child no longer needs the breast; it rests simply because the mother is near. Presence, not provision, is enough.

"Israel, put your hope (yaḥēl) in the LORD both now and forevermore" (v.3). One verse of national application. David’s personal humility becomes the nation’s counsel: hope in the LORD. The smallest psalm of the Ascents (3 verses) carries one of the deepest theologies: contentment through chosen smallness.

themes.note: Faith peaks: chosen humility and contentment. The weaned child: the Psalter’s most intimate image of trust. Three verses, infinite depth.
